title: Backend Diagnostics Playbook
content: This diagnostic guide provides a structured method to identify, isolate, and resolve backend service issues quickly and safely. Start with incident triage: capture timestamp, affected endpoints, user impact, error rate, and recent deploy/config changes. Confirm scope by checking whether failures are global, region-specific, tenant-specific, or limited to a single dependency. Health verification should include API liveness/readiness probes, database connectivity, cache reachability, message queue lag, and external provider status. Use correlation IDs to trace a failing request across gateway, service, worker, and storage layers. Review key signals first: p95/p99 latency, 5xx and 4xx distributions, timeout counts, saturation metrics (CPU, memory, file descriptors, thread pools), and retry storm indicators. For data-layer diagnosis, inspect slow query logs, lock waits, deadlocks, replication lag, connection pool exhaustion, and recent schema migrations. For async pipelines, validate consumer offsets, poison message handling, retry/dead-letter queue growth, and idempotency protections. Compare current behavior against baseline dashboards to detect regressions in throughput, latency, and resource usage. Inspect structured logs for high-cardinality error fingerprints, but ensure sensitive values are redacted. If a recent deployment is suspect, perform controlled rollback or canary disablement while preserving forensic artifacts. Apply mitigations in order of safety: traffic shaping/rate limits, feature-flag disable, dependency failover, queue backpressure controls, then rollback. After stabilization, run root-cause analysis using timeline reconstruction, contributing factors, and detection gaps. Produce actionable follow-ups: code fix, test coverage additions, alert tuning, runbook updates, and ownership with deadlines. Exit criteria for closure should include sustained metric recovery, no recurring alerts over a defined window, and verified customer-impact resolution.
